---
title: "Participant Setup Guide"
---

Welcome! Before the workshop starts, please work through this page. It should
take 30–45 minutes, most of which is waiting for installs. The goal: by the
end, you run one test script and everything prints PASS.

If anything fails and 15 minutes of trying doesn't fix it, send me an email!

## 1. Accounts you need

| Account | Needed for | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| [GitHub](https://github.com) | getting course materials | free |
| OpenRouter API key | all LLM labs (Days 3–5) | **arrives by email from me** — you don't need to sign up |
| [my.telegram.org](https://my.telegram.org) *(optional)* | live Telegram collection in Lab 2 | free |
| [ACLED](https://acleddata.com/register/) *(optional)* | live event-data download in Lab 8 | free for academic use |

The two optional accounts unlock the "live" versions of two labs. Every lab
also runs fully from cached data shipped with the course, so skipping them
costs you nothing essential.

**Your OpenRouter key:** I will email each of you an API key with a spending
cap during the workshop. Treat it like a password: don't commit it
to git or share it with others. The labs read it from an
environment variable or prompt you for it. The worst thing that could happen is that someone else will use up your credits, but each one is capped, so I won't get a bill for hundreds of dollars.

**Telegram (optional):** if you want to collect Telegram data live, you need a
Telegram account and API credentials. Log in at
[my.telegram.org](https://my.telegram.org) → "API development tools" → create
an app (any name). Note the `api_id` and `api_hash`. This is free and takes
five minutes; the awkward part is that it requires a phone number attached to
a Telegram account.

## 2. Pick your computing environment

Choose **one** of the following. If you're unsure, choose Colab

### Option A: Google Colab (free tier is fine)

No installation needed. Each lab notebook has a setup cell that detects Colab and
installs what it needs. You'll need a Google account and you should be
comfortable with Colab's quirk of forgetting installed packages between
sessions (our setup cells handle this).

### Option B: Local conda environment

If you prefer working on your own machine:

```bash
# clone the repository into a directory you want to work in
git clone https://github.com/ahalterman/icpsr-pipeline.git
cd icpsr-pipeline
# create and activate a conda environment
conda create -n icpsr python=3.11 -y
conda activate icpsr
# install the required packages
pip install -r setup/requirements.txt
```

The geospatial packages (`geopandas`, `rioxarray`) are the most likely to cause problems;
if `pip` fails on your machine, try `conda install -c conda-forge geopandas
rioxarray` instead.

## 3. Run the test script

From the repo root (Option B; on Colab, run the provided
`test_environment` notebook instead):

```bash
export OPENROUTER_API_KEY=sk-or-...   # the key from my email
python setup/test_environment.py
```

The script checks three things: package imports, a one-token call to
OpenRouter (cost: well under a cent), and that every cached dataset loads.

**Screenshot the output and email it to me**, whether or not everything
passed. That screenshot is your ticket to a smooth Day 1.

## 4. Capstone: start thinking

On Day 1 you'll declare a measurement target for the week-long capstone
project. The default menu, all on the Ukraine war: **civilian harm**,
**territorial control**, **infrastructure damage**, or **information
operations**. You may also bring your own topic (any region, any data source)
if you can describe what you want to *measure* in one sentence. You don't need to do have
a different case, but if you're thinking about something for a paper, dissertation, etc, I strongly recommend you work on that (it'll be more engaging and more useful).
